source.title: Event Boundaries Trigger Rapid Memory Reinstatement of the Prior Events to Promote Their Representation in Long-Term Memory

confidence_rationale: Direct human EEG evidence for event-boundary-triggered reinstatement, supporting (a) event-segment granularity for schema regions, (b) event boundaries as anchor-reset triggers parallel to V_s drift, (c) broadcast-trigger functional signature at chunk closure (relevant to MECH-287). Correlational rather than causal; localisation limited.
failure_signatures: If the substrate uses V_s drift as the sole anchor-reset trigger, it will miss the boundary-detection-driven trigger pathway that biology uses for sudden context shifts.
EEG cannot localise the reinstatement to hippocampus vs neocortex specifically; the broadcast-trigger interpretation is functional, not anatomical.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Event boundaries trigger rapid (200-800 ms) reinstatement of the just-encoded sequence pattern in human EEG. Reinstatement strength predicts subsequent ability to bind across boundaries in long-term memory, and is specific to context shifts following episodic content.
mapping.ree_translation: Event-segment is a natural granularity for schema regions in the substrate. Boundary detection is a candidate trigger for MECH-269 anchor reset, parallel to V_s drift. The reinstatement pattern matches the functional profile expected of MECH-287 broadcast trigger at chunk closure.
mapping.mapping_caveat: EEG cannot pinpoint hippocampus vs neocortex source; correlational rather than causal manipulation; laboratory task with explicit context shifts may exaggerate boundary signal vs naturalistic experience.
mapping.source_context: Human EEG pattern-similarity analysis during sequential learning with embedded context shifts; within-subject effects.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality; mapping fidelity strong for the boundary-as-trigger claim. Transfer risk moderate due to EEG localisation limits and laboratory paradigm.
